title: 5 4 Resonance
body:
5.4 FORCED OSCILLATIONS AND RESONANCE

When simple periodic forcing is added to the mechanical or electrical system studied earlier, we have to solve an equation like

my 00 + cy 0 + ky = F0 sin ŌČt (1)

where, as before, m > 0, c > 0, k > 0. This models a mechanical system driven by a periodic force with frequency ŌČ. When the frequency of the periodic force matches the natural frequency of the system, the system can start to resonate. This is a critical issue when dealing with real-world systems.

We saw in the previous lecture that the method of undetermined coefficient doesn‚Äôt always work. Whenever your natural guess for yp (t) is already a solution of the homogeneous problem, we need to generate a new trial solution by multiplying by t. If this is also a solution of the homogeneous problem, we just multiply by t again.

Example 1 Solve the second order differential equation

y 00 + 4y 0 = 12e‚ąí4t

and write down the long term steady state solution.

F y = A + Be‚ąí4t ‚ąí 3te‚ąí4t , y ‚Üí A as t ‚Üí ‚ąě F To understand what‚Äôs going on here we return to the physical system described earlier: a mass on a spring.

FORCED OSCILLATIONS

R = -ky V = y‚Äô

y 0 Fric = -cy‚Äô

Recall that this system was governed by the ODE my 00 + cy 0 + ky = 0 where m (mass), c (damping coefficient) and k (spring constant) are all positive. We now add a periodic forcing function to the RHS which drives the entire system with an additional periodic force with an amplitude F0 and a frequency ŌČ. The ODE is then

my 00 + cy 0 + ky = F0 sin wt

We will now show that a tiny change to the forcing function can have dramatic consequences to the solution of the ODE.

Example 2 A forced vibrating system is represented by

y 00 + 9y = 60 sin(2t)

where r(t) = sin(2t) is the driving force and y is the displacement from the equilibrium position. Find a formula for the motion of the system by solving the differential equation.

F y = A cos(3t) + B sin(3t) + 12 sin(2t) F

The first part of the solution is the homogeneous piece, sines and cosines vibrating at the natural frequency of the system (3). The second part of the solution is the particular solution, which matches the frequency of the forcing function (2).

Example 3 Solve the differential equation

y 00 + 9y = 60 sin(3t)

F y = A cos(3t) + B sin(3t) ‚ąí 10t cos(3t) F

Figure 1: Solutions to y 00 + 9y = F (t) for F (t) = 60 sin(2t) and F (t) = 60 sin(3t).

All we have done is change the forcing frequency from 2 to 3! But this small change means that the forcing frequency is now the same as the natural frequency. This leads to resonance of the system (it hums!) and unstable growth of the solution with time.

Resonance is a very important phenomenon in real-world systems. Sometimes it is a good thing, for example if you want to amplify a radio signal from a distance satellite. But in engineering problems it can lead to catastrophic failure of buildings or bridges if they are forced (by an earthquake, for example) at a frequency that matches their natural frequency of vibration.

Let us now see what happens as the system approaches resonance:

my 00 + cy 0 + ky = F0 sin ŌČt where, as before, m > 0, c > 0 and k > 0. We have already discussed the homogeneous solution (no forcing). We now look for a particular solution of the form

yp = a cos ŌČt + b sin ŌČt. You can show that a = m(ŌČ02 ‚ąí ŌČ 2 )F0 /‚ąÜ and b = ŌČcF0 /‚ąÜ where k 2 ŌČ02 = and ‚ąÜ = m2 ŌČ02 ‚ąí ŌČ 2 + ŌČ 2 c2 . m Alternately, you can show that the particular solution can be written in the form

yp = őė cos(ŌČt ‚ąí őī) with

F02 ŌČc őė2 =and tan őī = 2 . ‚ąÜ m(ŌČ0 ‚ąí ŌČ 2 ) In the case of undamped oscillations (c = 0), the complete solution is F y = A cos ŌČ0 t + B sin ŌČ0 t + cos ŌČt, m(ŌČ02 ‚ąí ŌČ 2 ) p for ŌČ 6= ŌČ0 . This is the sum of free oscillations with natural frequency ŌČ0 = k/m and forced oscillations with forcing frequency ŌČ. As ŌČ ‚Üí ŌČ0 , the amplitude of the forced oscillation gets larger and larger until the solution breaks down at ŌČ0 = ŌČ. In that case the solution is instead F t sin ŌČ0 t. y = A cos ŌČ0 t + B sin ŌČ0 t + 2mŌČ0 Thus, the system responds with a linearly increasing amplitude when it is forced at its natural frequency ŌČ0 .

In the case of damped oscillations (c > 0), we saw that the solutions of the homogeneous equation decay to zero as t ‚Üí ‚ąě. These are called the transients of the system. When they die out, we are left with the directly forced response

yp = őė cos(ŌČt ‚ąí őī) with F0 ŌČc őė= p and tan őī = . m2 (ŌČ02 ‚ąí ŌČ 2 )2 + ŌČ 2 c2 m(ŌČ02 ‚ąí ŌČ 2 ) So we see that the combination of mass m, damping c, and the restoring force constant k can be used to decrease or increase the response amplitude őė compared with the forcing amplitude F0 . In fact, by carefully tuning these parameters, weak signals can be magnified (for example, a radio receiver) and potentially dangerous resonances can be cancelled. An example of the latter is the 660 tonne steel mass damper mounted in the top of the Taipei 101 building, protecting it from typhoons and earthquakes. You can now do Q 85c,86,87
